coredata.dc:description: The photocatalytic properties and visible light absorption of boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride (B-g-C3N4) have garnered significant interest in recent years due to their potential applications in environmental remediation and energy conversion. However, enhancing the separation of electron-hole pairs has posed a notable challenge in optimizing the performance of this material. In this study, we synthesized B-g-C3N4 and integrated it with highly effective graphene quantum dots (GQD) to create a novel photocatalyst known as GQD/B-g-C3N4, derived from sweet lime peels. This environmentally friendly approach not only utilizes natural waste but also leverages the photocatalytic efficiency by improving light absorption and charge separation efficiency. The metal-free design of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst addresses environmental concerns linked to conventional metal-based catalysts, making it a sustainable alternative for direct CH arylation reactions under solar light. The GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst demonstrate exceptional stability by showing reusability across 4 cycles with minimal catalytic activity loss. Moreover, its potential to drive vital reactions for synthesizing heteroarenes which are key components in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals emphasizes its industrial significance. This study advances green chemistry and renewable energy efforts, providing an eco-friendly and efficient solution for fine chemical synthesis and environmental restoration.

XPS spectrum of GQDs and GQD@B-gC3N4 photocatalyst.
Thermogravimetric (TGA) and BET studies of (a) GQDs, (b) GQD/B-g-C3N4, (c) GQDs, and (d) GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst.

(a) 3-D structure of graphene quantum dots decorated boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride (GQD/B-g-C3N4) photocatalyst. The FESEM images of GQDs (b) GQDs along with elemental mapping (d) C1s and (e) O1s. The FESEM images of GQD/B-g-C3N4 (c) along with elemental mapping (f) C1s, (g) O1s, (h) N1s, and B1s, respectively.

Reaction conditions: GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst, 3 mL DMSO, 1 eq. 4-methoxybenzenediazonium tetrafluoroborate, thiophene, illuminated under solar light for 6 h in inert atmosphere at room temperature (eq. = equivalent).
Fabrication of newly designed graphene quantum dots decorated boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride as a photocatalyst for CH bond activation.
Energy level diagram of GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst showcasing transference of solar-driven electron for photocatalytic CH arylation.
Based on our findings, we propose a plausible chemical mechanism for the photocatalytic CH bond arylation occurring between compounds 1a and 2a in the presence of the GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The photocatalytic properties and visible light absorption of boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride (B-g-C3N4) have garnered significant interest in recent years due to their potential applications in environmental remediation and energy conversion. However, enhancing the separation of electron-hole pairs has posed a notable challenge in optimizing the performance of this material. In this study, we synthesized B-g-C3N4 and integrated it with highly effective graphene quantum dots (GQD) to create a novel photocatalyst known as GQD/B-g-C3N4, derived from sweet lime peels. This environmentally friendly approach not only utilizes natural waste but also leverages the photocatalytic efficiency by improving light absorption and charge separation efficiency. The metal-free design of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst addresses environmental concerns linked to conventional metal-based catalysts, making it a sustainable alternative for direct CH arylation reactions under solar light. The GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst demonstrate exceptional stability by showing reusability across 4 cycles with minimal catalytic activity loss. Moreover, its potential to drive vital reactions for synthesizing heteroarenes which are key components in pharmaceuticals and agrochemicals emphasizes its industrial significance. This study advances green chemistry and renewable energy efforts, providing an eco-friendly and efficient solution for fine chemical synthesis and environmental restoration.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: The quest for innovative photocatalysts has garnered considerable interest within the scientific community due to their wide-ranging applications in energy conversion and environmental remediation. The global demand for sustainable energy solutions and environmental remediation technologies underscores the urgency of developing efficient, eco-friendly photocatalysts. Traditional photocatalysts often rely on metal-based systems, which can be expensive and environmentally detrimental. Among the various materials being investigated, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride (B-g-C3N4) have emerged as highly promising candidates. These materials are particularly attractive for their unique properties, which can significantly enhance photocatalytic performance, especially in metal-free catalytic processes. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are zero-dimensional nanomaterials derived from graphene, typically characterized by dimensions ranging from 1 to 10 nano-meters [1]. Their small size coupled with quantum confinement effects and edge states endows GQDs with remarkable electronic, optical, and chemical properties [2]. This unique set of characteristics such as high surface area and tunable electronic properties makes GQDs suitable for a multitude of applications, including sensors, biomedical imaging, and particularly, photocatalysis [3,4]. One of the most striking features of GQDs is their strong photoluminescence, which arises from quantum effects and plays a critical role in facilitating energy transfer and enhancing light absorption—two essential components in photocatalytic reactions [5].

In addition to their exceptional photoluminescent properties, GQDs' high surface-to-volume ratio enhances catalytic efficiency by improving interactions with reactants, thereby accelerating reaction rates [6]. Despite these inherent advantages, the photocatalytic effectiveness of GQDs often requires the incorporation of other materials to fully realize their potential. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free semiconductor composed of carbon and nitrogen atoms, exhibits a layered structure that resembles graphite [7]. Its suitable band gap of approximately 2.7 eV makes it an attractive photocatalyst due to its ability to absorb visible light [8]. The introduction of boron into the g-C3N4 lattice, resulting in boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride (B-g-C3N4), alters its electronic characteristics, enhancing photocatalytic performance through improved visible light absorption and charge carrier dynamics [9,10]. Boron doping modifies the electronic structure of g-C3N4, increasing its visible light absorption and improving the dynamics of charge carriers within the material. The incorporation of boron introduces additional electronic states in the band gap, which promotes the efficient separation of photoinduced electron-hole pairs. This improved charge separation is essential for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of the material, as it facilitates the reaction processes that occur during photocatalysis [11]. In addition to enhancing light absorption and charge separation, boron doping significantly boosts the stability and chemical reactivity of g-C3N4 [11–22].

Studies have demonstrated that B-g-C3N4 exhibits superior photocatalytic performance compared to its undoped counterpart in various reactions, including the degradation of organic pollutants and the reduction of carbon dioxide [23,24]. These improvements highlight the potential of B-g-C3N4 as an effective photocatalyst for addressing environmental challenges and advancing sustainable energy technologies. To further enhance photocatalytic performance, researchers have explored the combination of g-C3N4 with graphene quantum dots (GQDs), resulting in the formation of nanohybrids. The integration of GQDs onto B-g-C3N4 nanosheets capitalizes on the complementary properties of these materials. GQDs possess exceptional light-harvesting capabilities and can improve charge transfer processes when paired with B-g-C3N4. This collaboration enhances light absorption, increases charge separation efficiency, and ultimately boosts the overall catalytic performance of the hybrid material [25].

Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have emerged as valuable components in photocatalytic applications due to their exceptional electronic and optical characteristics [26–30]. When integrated with boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride (B-g-C3N4), GQDs can effectively function as electron sinks by absorbing photoexcited electrons [31]. This interaction significantly reduces the likelihood of electron-hole pair recombination, a common challenge that often undermines the efficiency of photocatalytic processes. By effectively minimizing recombination, the stability and overall photocatalytic activity of the composite material are enhanced, providing a more efficient system for various photocatalytic reactions. The metal-free photocatalysts, such as GQDs and B-g-C3N4, represent a sustainable and cost-effective alternative. This study delves into their innovative applications, addressing critical gaps in the field and paving the way for advanced photocatalytic technologies.

Recent research has shown that GQD/B-g-C3N4 nanohybrids exhibit superior photocatalytic performance compared to their individual components. The formation of a heterojunction between B-g-C3N4 and GQDs is pivotal in this enhancement. This architectural design not only increases the absorption of visible light but also promotes effective charge separation and transfer, which are critical factors in achieving high photocatalytic efficiency [32]. This dynamic interplay between the components leads to a more robust and effective photocatalytic system. The advantages of GQD/B-g-C3N4 nanohybrids have been particularly evident in several photocatalytic reactions. For instance, studies have reported significant improvements in carbon dioxide reduction, the degradation of organic dyes, and hydrogen evolution processes [7,11,30,33–36]. These applications underscore the versatility and potential of these nanohybrids to address pressing environmental challenges, such as pollution and renewable energy production [37,38].

Moreover, the unique properties of GQDs, including their small size, high surface area, and tuneable bandgap, contribute to enhanced light-harvesting capabilities. This enables the GQD/B-g-C3N4 system to utilize a broader spectrum of sunlight, increasing its applicability in solar-driven photocatalytic processes. As research continues to explore the optimal synthesis and functionalization of these nanohybrids, the insights gained are expected to lead to even greater efficiencies and novel applications. While the development of GQD/B-g-C3N4 nanohybrids marks a significant advancement in photocatalytic materials, several challenges remain to be addressed. One crucial issue is achieving precise control over the synthesis process to ensure a uniform distribution of GQDs and optimal interaction with B-g-C3N4. This uniformity is vital for maximizing the efficiency of the composite and enhancing its overall photocatalytic performance [39].

Additionally, for these materials to be viable for practical applications, it is essential to solve scalability challenges in the synthesis process and ensure the long-term stability of the photocatalysts under operational conditions. Future research should focus on refining the synthesis parameters to improve the quality and functionality of these nanohybrids. Investigating various doping strategies could provide further insights into enhancing their photocatalytic properties. A deeper understanding of the interfacial processes between GQDs and B-g-C3N4 will also be critical for optimizing performance. Moreover, exploring the potential of these nanohybrids across a wider range of photocatalytic reactions could broaden their applicability and facilitate their use in industrial settings [40]. The combination of graphene quantum dots with boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride presents a promising opportunity for developing metal-free photocatalysts. The unique properties of these materials, when integrated into nanohybrid systems, can lead to significant improvements in photocatalytic activity, paving the way for numerous applications in environmental remediation and energy conversion. This study emphasizes the critical role of GQD/B-g-C3N4 nanohybrids as next-generation photocatalysts. The insights provided here not only showcase their innovative potential but also address the pressing necessity of advancing sustainable and eco-friendly technologies.

As research in this field progresses, it is anticipated that continued exploration and innovation will unveil new opportunities and address existing challenges, driving the development of novel solutions in photocatalytic technology. Together, these advancements will contribute to the advancement of sustainable practices and environmentally friendly technologies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Materials
ce:section.ce:para: Ethylene glycol, 4-Methoxy benzene diazonium tetrafluoroborate, thiophene, and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) have been purchased from Sigma-Aldrich and these chemicals were used without purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Methods
ce:section.ce:para: FT-IR spectra have been acquired by using a Perkin Elmer Spectrum IR Version 10.6. UV–Vis absorption spectra have been measured by using a Shimadzu UV-1900 UV–visible spectrophotometer. XPS analyses have been conducted on the X-Ray001 400 μm-FG ON instrument. X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis has been executed on the D5000 diffractometer. Thermogravimetric analysis has been carried out on the TGA 4000 instrument. The morphological study has been performed through the JSM-IT 500 model by JEOL. Cyclic voltammetry measurements have been carried out on the Metrohm Autolab PGSTAT204 instrument. SEM, EDX along with elemental mapping was recorded by JSM 6490 LV (constructed by JEOL, Japan). Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (BET) analysis was performed using the Quantachrome Autosorb iQ3 instrument, High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was analysed by UC-EF-TEM operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) from dry sweet lime peels
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was conducted using dry sweet lime peels as a low-cost and sustainable precursor. Initially, the dried sweet lime peels were processed into a fine powder through a grinding technique, resulting in powdered material rich in carbon content suitable for GQD formation. In a typical synthesis procedure, 10 g of the prepared sweet lime peel powder were combined with 100 ml of ethylene glycol in a dried 250 ml round-bottom flask. The mixture was subjected to reflux at a controlled temperature of 200 °C for an extended period of 12 h. This thermal treatment facilitates the carbonization of the organic material and the subsequent formation of graphene quantum dots. The progress of the reaction was monitored by observing a colour change in the solution, which transformed into a dark brown hue, indicating the successful synthesis of GQDs [41].

After the reflux process, the resultant dark brown solution, which contains the synthesized GQDs, was subjected to centrifugation to facilitate the separation of the GQD material from any undigested solid residues and by-products. The centrifugation process was carried out at an appropriate speed for 15 min, ensuring effective sedimentation of the GQDs. Following centrifugation, the GQDs were purified through a washing step using additional ethylene glycol. This cleaning procedure helps remove residual impurities and unreacted materials, thereby enhancing the purity and stability of the GQDs. This method not only highlights an effective approach to the synthesis of graphene quantum dots from agricultural waste but also contributes to the development of green chemistry practices by utilizing renewable resources (Scheme 1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of highly efficient GQD@B-gC3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst, which combines GQDs B-gC3N4, was accomplished through a hydrothermal treatment process. This method facilitates the effective interaction between GQDs and B-gC3N4, leading to enhanced photocatalytic properties. To initiate the fabrication, 500 mg of B-gC3N4 powder was mixed with 100 mg of GQDs in a solution of 50 mL of deionized water. This mixture was agitated thoroughly for 30 min at room temperature to ensure uniform dispersion of the GQDs within the B-gC3N4 medium. Proper mixing is crucial for achieving a homogenous material that maximizes the interaction between the components.

Following the initial mixing phase, the combined solution was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and subjected to hydrothermal conditions at 150 °C for a duration of 4 h. This heating process allows for the intercalation of GQDs into the B-gC3N4 matrix, enabling the formation of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 nanocomposite [42]. The choice of temperature and time is critical, as it influences the structural integrity and electronic properties of the resulting photocatalyst. After hydrothermal treatment, the resultant material was carefully washed with distilled water to remove any unreacted precursors and impurities. The purity of the final product is essential for achieving optimal photocatalytic activity. Following the washing process, the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst was dried in an oven at 100 °C for 8 h, yielding approximately 450 mg of the final product, representing a yield of 75 % (Scheme 2).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization of GQDs and GQD@B-gC3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The characterization of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the composite photocatalyst GQD/B-g-C3N4 was conducted using several analytical techniques, prominently including UV–visible absorption spectroscopy and electrochemical analysis. The results from these analyses provide insight into the optical and electronic properties of the synthesized materials. As illustrated in Fig. 1a, the UV–visible absorption spectra reveal distinct differences between GQDs and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite [43,44] [24,25]. The incorporation of GQDs onto the B-gC3N4 matrix significantly enhances its light-harvesting capabilities. The presence of GQDs improves the photocatalytic performance of the composite, suggesting that the quantum dots act as effective light absorbers, extending the absorption spectrum and enabling better utilization of visible light. This enhancement is critical for photocatalytic applications, where efficient light absorption directly influences the material's activity under solar irradiation.

Further analysis was conducted to examine the electronic structure of the photocatalyst through electrochemical characterization. By identifying the peak positions for maximum oxidation (1.48 eV) and reduction (1.15 eV), we can assess the energy levels of the Highest Occupied Molecular Orbital (HOMO) and the Lowest Unoccupied Molecular Orbital (LUMO). As shown in Fig. 1b, the determined energy levels are −5.98 eV for the HOMO and − 3.35 eV for the LUMO. This characterization is essential for understanding the charge transfer mechanisms that occur during photocatalytic reactions. The calculated band gap for the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite is determined to be 2.63 eV, which aligns closely with the optical band gap assessed through UV–visible absorption spectroscopy. This correspondence validates the model used for band gap determination and confirms the effectiveness of the synthesis method employed. A band gap in this range is particularly advantageous for photocatalytic activity since it allows the material to absorb a significant portion of the visible light spectrum, thus enhancing its potential for practical applications in environmental remediation and energy conversion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization and characterization of GQDs and GQD/B-g-C3N4: Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy
ce:section.ce:para: The functional groups present on the surface of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were examined using Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy, as depicted in Fig. 1c. The FT-IR spectrum of GQDs reveals four notable absorption peaks located at 3332, 2926, 1712, and 1598 cm−1. The absorption band at 3332 cm−1 is indicative of the stretching vibration associated with hydroxyl (OH) groups, demonstrating the presence of surface hydroxyl functionalities that contribute to the material's hydrophilicity. Additionally, the peak at 2926 cm−1 corresponds to the CH stretching vibrations, further supporting the organic nature of the GQDs.

The peaks at 1712 cm−1 and 1598 cm−1 are assigned to carbonyl (C=O) stretching and CC stretching vibrations, respectively, further confirming the presence of unsaturated bonds and functional groups on the GQDs' surface. The predominance of these functional moieties is significant as they enhance the water solubility and biocompatibility of the GQDs, making them suitable for various applications, including in biomedical fields and environmental remediation. However, the FT-IR spectrums of B-gC3N4 shows peaks at 1400 cm−1 and a wide peak at 3300 cm−1 corresponds to BO stretching and OH stretching respectively. Also, the triazine network present in B-gC3N4 shows stretching at 3300 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 corresponds to NH and CN stretching. In this contrast, the FT-IR spectrum of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst indicates [43,44] a broad absorption band in the range of 3000–3600 cm−1, which is associated with the stretching vibrations of NH and OH groups. Such broad bands suggest a strong hydrogen bonding network that may contribute to the stability and functionality of the photocatalyst. A distinct CH stretching band was observed at 2988 cm−1, signifying the preservation of organic functionalities within the composite.

Furthermore, the spectral data collected below 2000 cm−1 provide evidence for the presence of conjugated CN heterocycles in the range of 1100–1600 cm−1, alongside tri-s-triazine units that appear at 802 cm−1. These findings reinforce the notion that even after the incorporation of boron and the loading of GQDs onto B-gC3N4, the structural integrity of the native graphitic carbon nitride framework remains largely intact. The retention of these structural characteristics is crucial as it implies that the photocatalytic properties of g-C3N4 are preserved, or potentially enhanced, in the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite. This stability is essential for its effectiveness in photocatalytic applications, as any significant alteration in structure could adversely affect the material's performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray diffraction analysis of GQDs and GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns for graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite photocatalyst are presented in Fig. 1d. The XRD analysis reveals a distinct and sharp characteristic peak at 22.6 degrees, which corresponds to the (002) diffraction plane of GQDs. This peak indicates the formation of a graphitic structure derived from the carbonization process of sweet-lime powder, as previously reported in literature [45]. The presence of this defined peak is indicative of well-ordered graphitic domains within the GQDs, suggesting effective carbonization that enhances their structural integrity.

Upon the incorporation of GQDs into the B-g-C3N4 matrix, the (002) peak for the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite shifts to a lower angle, observed at approximately 27.5 degrees. This shift signifies an increase in interlayer spacing within the structure, indicating that the GQDs have been successfully integrated into the B-g-C3N4 framework. Additionally, the sharper peak of the composite compared to that of pure GQDs suggests improved crystallinity in the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst. The enhanced crystallinity may contribute to better charge separation and transport, factors that are crucial for optimizing photocatalytic activity.

The broadening of the XRD pattern observed for the GQDs could be attributed to the presence of various oxygenated functional groups on their surface, which introduces structural disorder. Such functionalities, while potentially affecting crystallinity, also play a significant role in enhancing the solubility and reactivity of GQDs, making them more effective in photocatalytic reactions. This structural characterization provided by XRD is essential for understanding the physical properties of the GQDs and their composite form. The observed changes in peak position and intensity not only confirm the successful synthesis of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite [45] but also suggest that the unique structural attributes of GQDs contribute positively to the overall properties of the photocatalyst.

ce:section.ce:section-title: X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis of GQD/B-g-C3N4
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 2 illustrates the results of the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis, which provides confirmation of the successful incorporation of graphene quantum dots (GQDs) onto boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride. Notable differences in elemental composition and bonding environments between the GQDs and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite [14,24,27–30,45] were observed, revealing detailed insights into their chemical interactions. For the pure GQDs, the C1s spectra exhibited characteristic peaks at 284.6 eV, corresponding to CC bonds, at 286.3 eV for CO functionalities, and at 288.7 eV related to O–C=O carboxyl groups. These findings indicate that GQDs possess a hydrophilic surface due to the presence of various oxygen-containing groups, a conclusion that aligns well with the FT-IR analysis conducted earlier. The oxygen (O1s) spectra for GQDs showed peaks at 532 eV (O=C) and 532.9 eV (OC), further confirming the functionalization and enhancing the solubility of GQDs in aqueous environments. In contrast, the C1s spectra of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite display peaks at 285.8 eV, 286.8 eV, and 288.6 eV, which correspond to C–NH, CB, and sp2 hybridized carbon (N–C=N) associated with boron-doped g-C3N4. Additionally, a peak at 284.6 eV corresponds to the graphitic carbon (C=C) from the GQDs, indicating that the graphitic structure remains intact after the composite formation [14,27–30].

The O1s spectra for the composite exhibited peaks at 532.0 eV (O=C) and 533.1 eV (OC), consistent with those found in the GQDs, indicating that the oxygen functionalities persist even after the doping process. Furthermore, the nitrogen (N1s) spectra demonstrated several peaks at 398.4 eV (N2c), 399.3 eV (B=N–C), 400.0 eV (N3c), and 400.8 eV (N-(C)2B), confirming the presence of various nitrogen species involved in bonding with both boron and carbon in the composite material. Additionally, the B1s spectra provided further evidence for boron inclusion within the structure, showing peaks at 191.2 eV (BC) and 191.6 eV (BN). These peaks suggest that boron atoms are incorporated into the g-C3N4 framework, interacting with carbon and nitrogen atoms to enhance the properties of the composite [14,27–30].

Collectively, the XPS results reveal that GQDs are effectively integrated into the B-gC3N4 structure, leading to new bond formations and verifying the creation of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite. The successful loading of GQDs not only changes the electronic properties of the B-gC3N4 but also enhances its photocatalytic capabilities through improved charge separation and transfer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thermal stability and BET analysis of GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: To assess the thermal stability and structural characteristics of the newly developed graphene quantum dots (GQDs) decorated with boron-doped graphitic carbon nitride, thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) studies were performed. The results provide insights into the thermal behavior and porosity of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst, critical factors influencing its performance in photocatalytic applications.

Fig. 1d presents the TGA thermograms for both the GQDs and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite under an inert atmosphere [14,27–30]. The thermogravimetric analysis of GQDs reveals three significant weight loss stages. The first stage, occurring between 25 and 110 °C, corresponds to a 5.3 % weight loss due to the evaporation of adsorbed water molecules on the surface of the GQDs. This initial loss suggests a relatively high level of moisture retention, which is typical for materials with surface functional groups. The second stage of weight loss, observed between 110 and 200 °C, results in a 4.3 % decrease in mass and is attributed to the gradual degradation of oxygen-containing groups, such as hydroxyl and carboxyl functionalities. These groups are essential for enhancing the hydrophilicity and reactivity of the GQDs but also contribute to their thermal instability, and the third weight loss occurs between 200 and 400 °C (nearly 70 % weight loss) and is linked to the breakdown of the carbon skeleton.

The most substantial weight loss, approximately 70 %, is noted between 200 and 400 °C and is linked to the decomposition of the carbon skeleton of the GQDs. This highlights the intrinsic thermal instability of the GQDs when subjected to higher temperatures, as the loss of structural integrity leads to a breakdown of their overall performance. In the case of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite, thermal decomposition begins at temperatures ranging from 300 to 350 °C. This degradation is primarily associated with the breakdown of the GQD components within the composite [14,27–30].

Notably, the thermal stability of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst is enhanced compared to pure GQDs, as evidenced by the higher initial degradation temperatures observed (Fig. 1d). At 700 °C, the total weight loss for the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite reached nearly 100 %. This finding indicates that the incorporation of B-gC3N4 significantly improves the thermal stability of the GQDs, likely due to the protective effects conferred by the carbon nitride matrix during thermal exposure.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms of GQDs and GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3c and d illustrates the nitrogen adsorption and desorption isotherms for both graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite. The determination of the adsorption characteristics for the synthesized GQDs and the decorated B-gC3N4 is somewhat challenging due to the significant reduction in surface area that occurs during the degassing process. To address this, a comparative analysis between GQD/B-g-C3N4 and GQDs is performed to elucidate the changes in porosity induced by annealing. According to the classification set forth by the International Union of Pure and Applied Chemistry (IUPAC), the isotherms exhibit H3 hysteresis, which is indicative of slit-like pore structures. This phenomenon suggests the presence of pore filling in the low-pressure region (see Fig. 3c). The presence of H3 hysteresis is characteristic of materials with a lamellar sheet-like mesoporous arrangement, a common feature in graphitic carbon nitride and its composites. This structural characteristic indicates that the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite effectively retains a mesoporous architecture that could facilitate enhanced catalytic activity due to improved accessibility for reactants.

Notably, the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst exhibits a higher Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) specific surface area (SSA) i.e. 7.751 m2/g compared to the pure GQDs i.e. 3.782 m2/g compared to the pure GQDs. This increase in surface area is a critical factor as it directly influences the photocatalytic performance by providing a greater area for interaction with ambient molecules. The enhanced specific surface area can be attributed to the effective integration of GQDs into the B-gC3N4 framework, which not only modifies the individual properties of the components but also optimizes the overall structure for specific applications. Furthermore, the pore size distribution analysis obtained from the isotherms, utilizing quenched solid density functional theory based on a slit-pore model, is presented in the inset of Fig. 3c. This analysis indicates that the GQDs predominantly feature larger pore sizes, whereas the typical pore diameter of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst is less than 4 nm. The smaller pore sizes in the composite are likely conducive to enhancing mass transport and reactivity during photocatalytic processes, affirming that the incorporation of GQDs leads to a more optimized structure for catalytic applications [24,46–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and elemental characterization of GQDs and GQD/B-g-C3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: The morphological characteristics of the synthesized graphene quantum dots (GQDs) and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite were analysed using field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM), as presented in Fig. 4 . This analysis reveals distinct differences in morphology between the two structures, highlighting the impact of the composite formation on their physical appearance. Initial observations of the FE-SEM images indicate that the GQDs exhibit a smooth, flat layered structure (Fig. 4a). This morphology is typical of GQDs and suggests a high degree of uniformity in their surface characteristics. In contrast, when GQDs (Fig. 4b)are integrated into the B-g-C3N4 framework to form the GQD/B-g-C3N4 (Fig. 4c) photocatalyst, a notable transformation occurs. The composite displays a rougher surface with an agglomerated structure [24,46–50], indicating that the coupling process alters the physical characteristics of the GQDs, enhancing their interaction within the composite matrix. This rough morphology is advantageous for photocatalytic applications as it can improve the surface area available for interactions with reactants which was also supported by HRTEM images (ESI, S1).

To gain further insights into the elemental composition of both GQDs and the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite, elemental mapping was performed. As anticipated, the elemental mapping of the GQDs confirmed the presence of carbon (C) and oxygen (O) (Fig. 4d and e). These elements are characteristic of GQDs and affirm their chemical composition. In contrast, the elemental analysis of the GQD@B-gC3N4 composite revealed a more complex distribution of elements, including boron (B), nitrogen (N), carbon (C), and oxygen (O) throughout the structure (Fig. 4e-h). This distribution illustrates successful incorporation of B and N into the framework, which are crucial for enhancing the photocatalytic properties of the material. The presence of boron and nitrogen is particularly significant as they can influence electronic properties and affect the reactivity of the photocatalyst. The morphological and elemental changes observed during the synthesis of GQD/B-g-C3N4 underscore the potential advantages of this composite for photocatalytic applications. The transformed structure may result in improved charge separation and enhanced photocatalytic efficiency due to the increased surface area and the introduction of new active sites [24,46–50].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photocatalytic activity of graphitic carbon nitride-based films for CH bond arylation
ce:section.ce:para: In this study, we focused on optimizing the photocatalytic activity of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite for the direct arylation of carbon‑hydrogen (CH) bonds using p-substituted aryl diazonium salts, specifically 4-methoxybenzenediazonium tetrafluoroborate, in reactions with thiophene. As depicted in Fig. 5 , these reactions were conducted at room temperature under solar light illumination. This approach represents a significant advancement in metal-free photocatalysis, offering a sustainable method to facilitate CH bond functionalization. The photocatalytic performance of the GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite shows a notable decrease when 1, 5, and 15 equivalents of thiophene are utilized in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), compared to the conditions set with 10 equivalents of thiophene in DMSO. This variation in activity is primarily attributed to the enhanced polarizability of thiophene in DMSO, resulting in more efficient interactions and reactivity of the thiophene moiety under the photocatalytic conditions. These findings resonate with prior studies, as per reported literature [50–58], which highlight the significant role that solvent choice plays in facilitating metal-free, visible-light-mediated CH arylation reactions. However, several experiments were performed to test the reusability and stability of GQD/B-g-C3N4 composite towards CH arylation reactions (ESI, S2).

Additionally, the mechanism underlying the enhanced photocatalytic activity may involve direct electron transfer processes stimulated by solar light illumination. During the photocatalytic CH arylation, the GQD/B-gC3N4 composite generates excited state electrons upon exposure to light, which can then participate in the arylation of the CH bond. This electron transfer mechanism is crucial for enhancing the reactivity of the substrate, leading to efficient product formation. Remarkably, under optimal conditions with 10 equivalents of thiophene in DMSO, the GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst achieved an impressive yield of 98 % alongside a selectivity of 99.65 % for the target arylated product. This exceptional performance underscores the effectiveness of the composite in facilitating direct CH arylation [31–40], particularly in the presence of sunlight, which acts as a sustainable energy source. The outcomes of this study suggest that the selection of specific reactant concentrations and solvent environment significantly impacts the photocatalytic efficiency of the GQD/B-gC3N4 system. Through careful optimization, we demonstrate that this material serves as a highly effective photocatalyst for CH arylation reactions, surpassing alternative conditions, including variations in thiophene equivalents, different solvents, and scenarios without light or photocatalyst, (ESI, S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Mechanistic study of direct CH arylation in the presence of newly designed GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst
ce:section.ce:para: Scheme 3 represents plausible mechanism of the electron transference on solar excitation of GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst. When exposed to solar energy, electrons from the GQD are transferred to the B-gC3N4 and then to its conduction band, where they drive the CH bond arylation reaction. The valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) positions of the GQD/B-gC3N4 composite are −5.98 eV and −3.35 eV, respectively, resulting in a band gap of 2.63 eV. This well-aligned band structure supports the mechanism of photocatalytic CH bond arylation and validates the proposed process. Moreover, the electron-rich nature of GQD enables it to efficiently transfer electrons to B-gC3N4, generating electron-hole pairs that are critical for initiating and sustaining the reaction.

As illustrated in the schematic representation (Scheme 4 ), the GQD/B-gC3N4 (designated as PC) acts as a photo-redox catalyst upon exposure to visible light, initiating the reaction process [47–51]. The mechanism begins with the excitation of the photocatalyst, leading to the formation of a singlet excited state (PC*). This singlet state undergoes intersystem crossing (ISC) to form a more stable triplet state (3PC*). The triplet state plays a vital role in facilitating a single electron transfer (SET) to the p-substituted aryl diazonium salt (1a), generating an aryl radical (1b) and converting the photocatalyst into its cationic form (PC+•). The aryl radical (1b) subsequently reacts with the heteroarene (e.g., thiophene, 2a) to produce a radical intermediate (2b). This intermediate undergoes one of two oxidation pathways. In the first pathway, it is oxidized by the cationic form of the photocatalyst (PC+•), which simultaneously regenerates the ground state photocatalyst (PC). In the second pathway, the radical intermediate is oxidized through a radical chain transfer mechanism involving aryl diazonium salts, sustaining the reaction process. The oxidized intermediate (2c) then undergoes deprotonation, restoring aromaticity and leading to the formation of the desired product (3a). Throughout the process, the photocatalyst is regenerated at the end of the cycle, maintaining its catalytic efficiency and enabling repeated use of the GQD/B-gC3N4 system without significant degradation. This efficient charge transfer and regeneration mechanism are critical to driving the photocatalytic reaction forward.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusions
ce:section.ce:para: In summary, we have successfully developed the GQD/B-gC3N4 composite, which features a covalent bonding structure. This innovative material has enabled, for the first time, the direct CH bond arylation under solar light using a non-metallic photocatalyst, marking a significant advancement in the field of photocatalysis. Our results demonstrate that the GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst offers excellent compatibility with various substituents possessing diverse functional groups, showcasing its versatile applications in chemical transformations. One of the most notable advantages of the GQD/B-gC3N4 system is its cost-effectiveness, combined with its environmentally friendly characteristics and inherent stability during photocatalytic reactions. These attributes make it an attractive alternative to traditional metal-based photocatalysts, which often come with higher costs and environmental concerns. Looking forward, our ongoing research is focused on broadening the applicability of GQD/B-gC3N4 in solar light-mediated reactions for various synthetic applications. The promising outcomes of our study underscore the significant potential of this photocatalyst in facilitating direct CH bond arylation, thus paving the way for the development of more sustainable and efficient methods in precision chemical synthesis. Finally, the GQD/B-gC3N4 photocatalyst represents a transformative step towards reducing reliance on conventional metal-based and powder photocatalysts, offering a more sustainable approach to organic synthesis.
